topic.name: American Civil War
topic.description: Key events from 1861-1865, including causes, major battles, and aftermath of the American Civil War.

events.title: Attack on Fort Sumter
events.description: Confederate forces bombarded Fort Sumter in Charleston Harbor, South Carolina, marking the beginning of the Civil War. After 34 hours of bombardment, Major Robert Anderson surrendered the fort.
events.significance: The attack on Fort Sumter unified the North and prompted Lincoln to call for 75,000 volunteers, officially beginning the Civil War.

events.title: First Battle of Bull Run
events.description: The first major land battle of the Civil War, fought near Manassas, Virginia. Confederate forces, reinforced by troops arriving by rail, routed the Union army.
events.significance: The Confederate victory shattered Northern hopes for a quick end to the war and demonstrated that the conflict would be long and costly.

events.title: Battle of Antietam
events.description: The bloodiest single-day battle in American history, with approximately 23,000 casualties. General McClellan's Army of the Potomac halted Lee's first invasion of the North.
events.significance: The Union victory gave Lincoln the opportunity to issue the Preliminary Emancipation Proclamation and kept Britain and France from recognizing the Confederacy.

events.title: Emancipation Proclamation
events.description: President Lincoln issued the Emancipation Proclamation, declaring all slaves in Confederate-held territory to be forever free, effective January 1, 1863.
events.significance: Transformed the war from a conflict to preserve the Union into a war to end slavery, preventing European intervention and allowing African Americans to serve in the Union Army.

events.title: Battle of Gettysburg
events.description: The largest battle of the Civil War and a turning point, fought over three days in southern Pennsylvania. Lee's second invasion of the North ended in a decisive Confederate defeat.
events.significance: The Union victory ended Confederate hopes of winning the war through a military victory in the North and marked the beginning of the Confederacy's decline.

events.title: Siege of Vicksburg
events.description: Union forces under General Grant besieged the Confederate fortress city of Vicksburg, Mississippi, gaining control of the Mississippi River after the city's surrender.
events.significance: The fall of Vicksburg gave the Union complete control of the Mississippi River, splitting the Confederacy in two.

events.title: Gettysburg Address
events.description: President Lincoln delivered a brief but powerful speech at the dedication of the Soldiers' National Cemetery at Gettysburg, redefining the purpose of the war.
events.significance: The address redefined the Civil War as a struggle for equality and democracy, becoming one of the most quoted speeches in American history.

events.title: Sherman's March to the Sea
events.description: General William T. Sherman led Union forces on a devastating march from Atlanta to Savannah, Georgia, destroying Confederate infrastructure and supplies.
events.significance: The march demonstrated the Union's ability to wage total war and helped break Southern morale and will to continue fighting.

events.title: Lee Surrenders at Appomattox
events.description: General Robert E. Lee surrendered the Army of Northern Virginia to General Ulysses S. Grant at Appomattox Court House, effectively ending the Civil War.
events.significance: Lee's surrender marked the end of major military operations and began the process of reuniting the nation.

events.title: Assassination of Abraham Lincoln
events.description: President Abraham Lincoln was shot by John Wilkes Booth while attending a play at Ford's Theatre. He died the following morning.
events.significance: Lincoln's assassination deeply affected the nation's mourning and complicated Reconstruction efforts under his successor, Andrew Johnson.

events.title: 13th Amendment Ratified
events.description: The 13th Amendment to the Constitution was ratified, formally abolishing slavery throughout the United States.
events.significance: The amendment permanently ended the institution of slavery in the United States and laid the groundwork for civil rights legislation.
